title: Six charts show how clean power was world's largest source of new energy in 2025 - Carbon Brief
gist: The Energy Institute's 2026 Statistical Review of World Energy finds wind and solar combined were the world's single largest source of new energy in 2025 (up 18.3% YoY), clean power met all global electricity demand growth, data centres made up only 2% of global electricity use and 15% of 2025's demand increase, and China now generates more electricity than the US, EU and India combined.

themes.theme: Wind and solar overtake fossil fuels as top growth source

insights.claim: Wind and solar combined were the world's largest single source of new energy in 2025, growing 18.3% year-on-year and, outside the Covid pandemic, this was the first year ever that combined wind and solar contributed more new energy than any individual fossil fuel.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A structural inflection point: renewables are now the marginal supplier of global energy growth, not just a fast-growing niche — reinforces long renewables-equipment and grid-integration investment theses over incremental fossil-fuel capacity additions.
insights.quote: it was also the first year ever in which wind and solar, when combined, contributed more new energy than any of the individual fossil fuels

insights.claim: Global energy supply rose 10 exajoules (1.7%) to surpass 600EJ for the first time in 2025, with every individual energy source — oil, coal, gas, nuclear, wind and solar, hydro and other renewables — hitting new all-time highs simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Simultaneous record highs across every fuel source shows growth is still additive rather than substitutive at the primary-energy level — investors should not assume renewables growth is yet displacing fossil-fuel volumes in absolute terms.
insights.quote: global energy supply reached a record high in 2025, climbing 10 exajoules (EJ, 1.7%) to more than 600EJ for the first time ever

insights.claim: Coal hit a new record of 166EJ in 2025, up 0.7% year-on-year and 2.8% above the 2014 level that had previously been seen as a potential peak for the fuel.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Directly refutes the 'peak coal was 2014' narrative with a hard data point — coal demand has structurally exceeded its prior presumed peak, a bearish signal for any investment thesis premised on imminent global coal decline.
insights.quote: coal hit a new record of 166EJ in 2025, up 0.7% from a year earlier and 2.8% above the level reached in 2014, which had been seen as a potential peak for the fuel

insights.claim: Over the past decade, wind and solar capacity expanded fivefold, while coal, oil and gas grew by only 6%, 9% and 21% respectively over the same period.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A 5x decade-long growth multiple for wind/solar versus single-digit-to-low-double-digit fossil fuel growth quantifies the scale of the renewables growth-rate advantage for portfolio construction across the energy sector.
insights.quote: Over the past decade, they expanded fivefold, while coal, oil and gas grew by 6%, 9% and 21%, respectively.
insights.locator: Wind and solar were biggest source of new energy in 2025

themes.theme: The 'primary energy fallacy' distorts fossil-fuel share

insights.claim: Fossil fuels made up a record-low 86.2% of global primary energy supply in 2025, but this 'total energy supply' metric overstates fossil fuels' real economic contribution because roughly two-thirds of the energy in fossil fuels is lost as waste heat during combustion, versus renewables where the metric counts electricity output directly.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: This 'primary energy fallacy' framework means headline fossil-fuel share statistics systematically understate how far along the electrification/decarbonisation transition actually is — investors relying on primary-energy shares alone are overestimating fossil fuel's real economic dominance.
insights.quote: This gives rise to the “primary energy fallacy”, which tends to “inflate…the perceived contribution of fossil fuels” and the difficulty of replacing them with low-carbon energy sources.

insights.claim: In the global transport sector, 105 units of primary energy (almost all oil) went in but only generated 20 units of useful transport 'energy services' — meaning less than 20% of transport primary energy actually moves people or goods, with the rest lost as waste heat.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: An 80%+ efficiency loss in oil-based transport energy is a concrete number underpinning the case that electrification (EVs) requires far less primary energy input for equivalent useful work — supports long-term EV/electrification demand-destruction thesis for oil.
insights.quote: less than 20% of the primary energy being used for transport actually ends up moving people or goods, while the remaining 80% was lost as waste heat

insights.claim: The Energy Institute dropped the 'substitution method' (which had counted renewable electricity as the equivalent fossil-fuel energy that would have been needed) from its statistical review starting in 2025, explicitly reasoning that primary energy demand will decrease as electrification and renewable generation share increase, since less input energy is needed to do the same useful work.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A methodology change by the industry's authoritative statistical review (successor to BP's review) formally acknowledges a shrinking-primary-energy-demand future under electrification — a signal that official global energy-demand growth forecasts may structurally decelerate as the energy mix shifts.
insights.quote: Put simply, in future we will need to supply less energy in the form of clean electricity to undertake the same amount of work as the equivalent energy supplies from fossil fuels.

insights.claim: The rise of nuclear power had already pushed the fossil-fuel share of global energy down to 91% by 1986, before the Chernobyl disaster halted further nuclear growth; it is only in the past decade that clean-energy sources resumed gaining share, driven by wind and solar.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Historical precedent shows a single catastrophic event (Chernobyl) stalled decarbonisation progress for roughly three decades — a cautionary data point on tail-risk fragility of energy-transition trajectories that investors should weigh when assessing nuclear-related political/safety risk today.
insights.quote: The rise of nuclear power had pushed the fossil-fuel share of global energy down to 91% as long ago as 1986, before the Chernobyl disaster pulled the plug on further growth.
insights.locator: Fossil fuels met a record-low 86.2% of global energy supply

themes.theme: Power sector: clean energy meets all demand growth

insights.claim: Wind, solar and a new nuclear-power record combined met all of the growth in global electricity demand in 2025; fossil generation was flat, with a small coal decline offset by a small gas rise, while wind and solar now generate more electricity than either hydro or nuclear.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: Fossil generation being flat while demand growth is entirely met by clean sources is the clearest evidence yet of a genuine (not just relative) displacement dynamic in the power sector specifically — bullish for continued clean-generation capex even without further fossil retirement.
insights.quote: combined with a new record for nuclear power, they met all of the growth in global electricity demand in 2025
insights.locator: Clean energy met all of global electricity growth in 2025

insights.claim: Global electricity generation increased by about 940 terawatt hours (3%) in 2025, roughly three times the UK's annual electricity demand.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A 940TWh annual demand increase (3x UK's total annual consumption) quantifies the scale of new generation capacity the world must add each year just to keep pace with demand growth — a benchmark for sizing renewables/grid capex requirements.
insights.quote: global electricity generation increased by some 940 terawatt hours (TWh, 3%), roughly three times the annual demand of the UK
insights.locator: Clean energy met all of global electricity growth in 2025

insights.claim: Data centres used 788TWh of electricity in 2025, up 130TWh from the prior year, accounting for only 2% of global electricity demand overall but 15% of the year's total electricity demand increase; electrification of industry, heat and transport is a far larger driver of demand growth than data centres.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A contrarian, evidence-based correction to the popular narrative that AI/data-centre power demand is the dominant driver of electricity growth — data centres are only 15% of the 2025 increase, meaning broader industrial/transport electrification is the bigger investable theme for grid and generation capacity.
insights.quote: these only make up 2% of all electricity use and 15% of the increase in 2025

insights.claim: China now generates more electricity than the US, EU and India combined, and electricity already makes up 30% of China's final energy supply in 2025, above the roughly 22% global average.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: China's electricity-generation scale exceeding three major economic blocs combined underlines its structural dominance in the global power-equipment, grid, and clean-tech manufacturing supply chain — a scale advantage difficult for competitors to replicate.
insights.quote: China now generates more electricity than the US, EU and India combined
insights.locator: China generates more power than the US, EU and India combined

insights.claim: China recently adopted a target of 35% electrification of final energy by 2030, and while much of its electricity growth has historically come from coal, enough clean-power growth occurred in 2025 to push coal generation down for the year.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A 2025 coal-generation decline within China's power mix despite historical coal-led growth is an early concrete signal that China's clean-power buildout has reached sufficient scale to actually crowd out coal generation, not just add alongside it — a bullish inflection for Chinese clean-power equipment investment.
insights.quote: there was enough growth of clean-power sources to push coal down last year
insights.locator: China generates more power than the US, EU and India combined

insights.claim: The COP31 presidency has called for a global goal of 35% of 'final' energy to come from electricity by 2035, versus a current global average of around 22%.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief
insights.so_what: A formally proposed global electrification target (35% by 2035, up from 22%) would, if adopted, become a benchmark against which national electrification/grid-investment plans and clean-tech capex commitments get measured at future COP negotiations.
insights.quote: The COP31 presidency has called for countries to back a global goal for 35% of “final” energy to come from electricity by 2035, against a global average today of around 22%.
insights.locator: China generates more power than the US, EU and India combined
